topics.label: Nouns
topics.definition: A noun is a naming word for a person, place, thing or idea.

examples.sentence: The dog ran across the field.
examples.explanation: Dog and field are nouns because they name things.

topics.label: Verbs
topics.definition: A verb shows an action or a state of being. Some verbs are auxiliary (helping) verbs.

examples.explanation: Is is an auxiliary verb and running is the main verb.

examples.sentence: The tall building touched the clouds.

topics.label: Adverbs
topics.definition: An adverb gives more information about a verb. It often tells us how, when or where.

examples.sentence: She ran quickly to the gate.
examples.explanation: Quickly tells us how she ran.

topics.label: Determiners
topics.definition: A determiner comes before a noun and shows which one or how many.

topics.label: Prepositions
topics.definition: A preposition shows the relationship between a noun and another word in the sentence.

examples.sentence: The cat slept under the table.
examples.explanation: Under shows the position of the cat.

topics.label: Subordinate Clauses
topics.definition: A subordinate clause gives extra information but cannot stand alone as a sentence.

examples.sentence: I stayed inside because it was raining.
examples.explanation: Because it was raining is a subordinate clause.

topics.label: Pronouns
topics.definition: A pronoun replaces a noun to avoid repetition.

examples.explanation: Her is a pronoun referring to Sarah.

topics.label: Conjunctions
topics.definition: A conjunction joins words, phrases or clauses.

examples.sentence: I wanted to go but it was raining.

topics.label: Active Voice
topics.definition: In an active sentence the subject performs the action. The verb is a straightforward doing word: The dog bit the postman. The dog (subject) does the biting.

examples.explanation: Bit is the active verb — the dog (subject) performs the action.

topics.label: Passive Voice
topics.definition: In a passive sentence the subject receives the action. We form the passive with was/were + past participle. The doer can be added with by: The postman was bitten by the dog.

examples.sentence: The postman was bitten by the dog.
examples.explanation: Was bitten is the passive verb phrase — the postman (subject) receives the action. The dog follows 'by'.

topics.label: Simple Present
topics.definition: The simple present tense describes actions happening now, regularly, or as a general truth. Regular verbs add -s or -es in the third person singular (e.g. runs, watches).

examples.sentence: She runs to school every morning.
examples.explanation: Runs is a simple present tense verb — it describes a regular, repeated action.

topics.label: Simple Past
topics.definition: The simple past tense describes a completed action in the past. Regular verbs add -ed (e.g. walked). Many common verbs are irregular (e.g. ran, ate, broke, wrote).

examples.sentence: She ran to school this morning.
examples.explanation: Ran is a simple past tense verb — it describes a completed action. Ran is the irregular past of run.

topics.label: Present Progressive
topics.definition: The present progressive (or present continuous) describes actions happening right now or around the present time. It is formed with am/is/are + verb + -ing. Example: I am playing.

examples.explanation: "Is writing" is the present progressive verb phrase — "is" (auxiliary) + "writing" (main verb + -ing).

topics.label: Past Progressive
topics.definition: The past progressive (or past continuous) describes actions that were in progress at a particular point in the past. It is formed with was/were + verb + -ing. Example: I was playing.

examples.sentence: She was singing when the bell rang.
examples.explanation: "Was singing" is the past progressive verb phrase — "was" (auxiliary) + "singing" (main verb + -ing).

topics.label: Present Perfect
topics.definition: The present perfect is formed with has or have + past participle. It links the past to the present — describing something that just happened, or that started in the past and still matters now.

examples.explanation: Has won is a present perfect verb phrase — has is the auxiliary verb and won is the past participle.

topics.label: Past Perfect
topics.definition: The past perfect is formed with had + past participle. It describes an action that was completed before another event in the past — the further-back past.

examples.sentence: By the time he arrived, she had won the race.
examples.explanation: Had won is past perfect — her winning happened before he arrived. We use had + past participle to show this earlier past event.

topics.label: Simple Future
topics.definition: The simple future describes actions that will happen after now. It is formed with will + base verb. Example: I will play. It can also be expressed with am/is/are going to + base verb.

examples.sentence: She will visit her grandparents tomorrow.
examples.explanation: "Will visit" is the simple future — "will" (modal auxiliary) + "visit" (base form of the verb, no -ing or -ed).

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses a comma correctly?
choices: After lunch we went to the park.
After lunch, we went to the park.
After, lunch we went to the park.
After lunch we, went to the park.
questions.answer: After lunch, we went to the park.

markScheme.modelAnswer: After lunch, we went to the park.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: In the sentence "The dog barked loudly at the postman", what word class is "loudly"?

markScheme.explanation: Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. "Loudly" tells us how the dog barked, so it modifies the verb "barked".

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses a colon correctly?
choices: She needed: milk, bread and eggs.
She needed three things: milk, bread and eggs.
She: needed milk, bread and eggs.
questions.answer: She needed three things: milk, bread and eggs.

markScheme.modelAnswer: She needed three things: milk, bread and eggs.
markScheme.explanation: A colon introduces a list or explanation. The clause before the colon must be able to stand alone as a complete sentence.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses a plural possessive apostrophe correctly?

markScheme.explanation: When a plural noun already ends in -s, the apostrophe goes after the s (boys'). "Boys's" is never correct in English.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: "By the time the lesson ended, she had finished her work." What tense is used?

markScheme.explanation: The past perfect uses "had" + past participle. It describes an action completed before another past action.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: What does the prefix "auto-" mean?

markScheme.explanation: "Auto-" comes from Greek, meaning "self". Examples: autobiography (writing about oneself), automatic (self-acting).

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Adding the suffix "-tion" to the verb "explain" creates which word class?

markScheme.explanation: The suffix "-tion" or "-ation" converts verbs into nouns. "Explain" becomes "explanation". Others: create → creation, decide → decision.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which word is a subordinating conjunction?

markScheme.explanation: Subordinating conjunctions (although, because, while, if…) join a main clause to a subordinate clause. "And", "but" and "or" are coordinating conjunctions.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence punctuates direct speech correctly?
choices: "Can we go to the park" asked Mia.
"Can we go to the park?" asked Mia.
"Can we go to the park?" Asked Mia.
Can we go to the park? asked Mia.
questions.answer: "Can we go to the park?" asked Mia.

markScheme.modelAnswer: "Can we go to the park?" asked Mia.
markScheme.explanation: A question inside speech ends with a question mark inside the closing inverted comma. The reporting verb ("asked") starts with a lower-case letter.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: "The book, which I borrowed from the library, was fascinating." What type of clause is "which I borrowed from the library"?

markScheme.explanation: A relative clause is introduced by a relative pronoun (who, which, that, whose) and gives more information about a noun. It is a type of subordinate clause.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which is an expanded noun phrase?

markScheme.explanation: An expanded noun phrase adds detail to a noun using adjectives or other modifiers. "The old, dusty book" expands the noun "book" with two adjectives.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses a semicolon correctly?
choices: She was tired; she went to bed early.
She was tired; going to bed.
She; was tired and went to bed.
questions.answer: She was tired; she went to bed early.

markScheme.modelAnswer: She was tired; she went to bed early.
markScheme.explanation: A semicolon joins two closely related independent clauses — both parts must be able to stand alone as sentences. "She was tired" and "she went to bed early" both can.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses the subjunctive mood correctly?
choices: If I was you, I would apologise.
If I were you, I would apologise.
If I am you, I would apologise.
If I be you, I would apologise.
questions.answer: If I were you, I would apologise.

markScheme.modelAnswer: If I were you, I would apologise.
markScheme.explanation: The subjunctive is used for hypothetical situations. "If I were you" (not "was") is correct — this is the standard subjunctive form in formal English.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which word is a determiner?

markScheme.explanation: Determiners introduce nouns and tell us which or how many. "The" (definite article) is a determiner. Others include: a, an, some, every, this, my, your.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which word uses the prefix "mis-" correctly to mean "wrongly"?

markScheme.explanation: The prefix "mis-" means wrongly or badly. "Misfortune" (bad luck) is a real word. The others are not real English words with this prefix.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: The suffix "-less" means:

markScheme.explanation: "-less" means "without". Examples: careless (without care), hopeless (without hope), speechless (without speech).

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses brackets for parenthesis correctly?
choices: My teacher (Mrs Jones gave us lots of homework.
My teacher (Mrs Jones) gave us lots of homework.
My teacher Mrs Jones) gave us lots of homework.
My teacher (Mrs Jones gave us) lots of homework.
questions.answer: My teacher (Mrs Jones) gave us lots of homework.

markScheme.modelAnswer: My teacher (Mrs Jones) gave us lots of homework.
markScheme.explanation: Parenthesis adds extra information and always comes in pairs — one opening and one closing bracket. Remove the bracketed section and the sentence must still make sense.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence is in the passive voice?
choices: The mouse was caught by the cat.
The cat is catching the mouse.
The cat had caught the mouse.
questions.answer: The mouse was caught by the cat.

markScheme.modelAnswer: The mouse was caught by the cat.
markScheme.explanation: In the passive voice, the subject receives the action. "The mouse was caught by the cat" uses "to be" + past participle. The active voice is "The cat caught the mouse."

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which word is a modal verb?

markScheme.explanation: Modal verbs express possibility, necessity, or ability: can, could, will, would, shall, should, may, might, must. They always come before a base verb.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence uses a hyphen correctly?

markScheme.explanation: Hyphens join compound adjectives placed before a noun. "A well-known author" needs a hyphen before the noun. "She is well known" (after the noun) does not.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: "She has been reading for two hours." What tense is this?

markScheme.explanation: The present perfect continuous uses "have/has been" + verb+ing. It describes an action that started in the past and is still continuing now.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which word contains the root meaning "to write"?

markScheme.explanation: The root "graph" comes from Greek, meaning "to write". "Paragraph" contains this root. Other examples: autograph, biography, photograph.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which pair are both coordinating conjunctions?

markScheme.explanation: Coordinating conjunctions join equal clauses. Remember FANBOYS: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So. "Because", "although", "while", "if" and "unless" are all subordinating.

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which sentence contains a relative pronoun?
choices: The girl who won the race smiled.
questions.answer: The girl who won the race smiled.

markScheme.modelAnswer: The girl who won the race smiled.
markScheme.explanation: Relative pronouns (who, which, that, whose, where) introduce relative clauses. "Who" in "the girl who won the race" refers back to "the girl".

questions.subject: gps
questions.stem: Which is the most formal version of this sentence?
choices: I can't do it cos I'm busy.
I cannot do it because I am busy.
I can't do this as I'm rather busy.
questions.answer: I cannot do it because I am busy.

markScheme.modelAnswer: I cannot do it because I am busy.
markScheme.explanation: Formal writing uses full words (cannot, I am) rather than contractions (can't, I'm), avoids slang (cos), and uses complete sentences.
